item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what the fuck what the fuck what the fuck what the fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the record, it was by Kalinin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved it, full of WTFery, up until the addenda turned it into predictable cliché horror movie tripe. I just can't upvote because of that. If they'd found the machine and it was perfectly normal and harmless, that would've been infinitely more scary and mysterious than what's there now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh great, just when I finally had the time to translate this skip to Spanish and congratulate the author over this, his o her account is deleted. Anyways here's my +1.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.
Fucking finally. Congrats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the horrendous dissonance between the technical machine instructions and the personal feeling of suicide notes, and the exploration log was very well-written and suspenseful, too… but I feel that the ultimate reveal was a little bit disappointing.

I didn't know what a twin screw extrusion machine was, but when I googled it, my first thought was 'that's going to end up grinding up humans, isn't it?' - and indeed it was, which was a tad disappointing. I was happy enough with that, though, but the face monster was entirely out of left-field (in that it wasn't completely bizarre - the rest of the article is made of 'what??', and then face monster felt a bit too boring) and then a chase back through a cave and the higher ups just sealing it off left me a little cold.

I think just the extrusion of ground up human would have been fine. It would have left the final thought of the article being 'why the hell is any of this happening?' rather than 'eek chase'.

But I still liked it a lot, and certainly enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know there are issues with this, but I liked the underlying narrative so much that I had to upvote anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anything that reminds me of Ted's Caving Page is a good thing.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Delightfully creepy, and not at ALL 'safe'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My sense of linguistic outrage has been sated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^ what I came to say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice, and creepy.

One thing though: how in Yog-Sothoth's name is this thingie "safe"? It's a spooky action at a distance that somehow makes suicidal folks relate an industrial machine's operating manual, and the machine itself, when located, turns out to have "spawn monster" as a security feature.

The machine and / or the effect responsible for altering the behavior of suicidal people appear to have capacity for independent action, so this thing is Euclid.

Also, the "spooky action at a distance" is apparently not constrained by physical boundaries, so it is an uncontained Euclid. At least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
were all the notes, or at least the ones included in the article, written in English? Because if not, the capitalization in the third wouldn't translate from Korean.

Notes were in Korean, and I have "feexed", as the kids say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made some changes to address some of the stuff Soulless brought up, mostly the issue with the Korean translation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that was a trip! Great narrative through the whole thing.

My one question: were all the notes, or at least the ones included in the article, written in English? Because if not, the capitalization in the third wouldn't translate from Korean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though the ultimate fate of the victims is kinda different, I do see the stylistic similarities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's leading up and there's completely forgoing putting needed information in there. If a grunt didn't need to know, it can be censored. You can at least mention that it has some significance. You don't have to mention of what specific importance it is but come on!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Putting the twin-screw extruder system after the rest of the article lets the detailed journey description lead up to something. I think putting it earlier would just ruin the tension.

Also, the twin-screw extruder system in the cave is Level 3 Clearance knowledge; it's not in the description because not every foundation grunt needs to know about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not unlike SCP-610 in the log of strange mutant biology. I would have to echo comments that the addition is unnecessary, as it does not add to the article. However, it does not subtract from it either, and so there is no real point in removing it, I would predict either action would still yield an SCP of similar upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think you should call a technical manual a "narrative". Maybe just call it a manual?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted voraciously.

(For the record, I liked all of the commas.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay now that I'm on a computer I can talk about why I dislike this article.

Ranting and Ripping - Click to Show!
The Show's Over Kids, go Home

SCP-1538-1

This isn't mentioned until the last paragraph in the description, but is still a very significant part of this article. It should be better noted that SCP-XXXX is a collective term for a bunch of suicide notes AND related artifacts, such as SCP-XXXX-1. These are structural issues but are very jarring to me, because SCP-XXXX-1 wasn't at all previously mentioned in the description other than the last paragraph. I understand if it is a relatively unimportant tidbit, but it's not at all. This is a significant detail that is shoved to the last part of the description. Worst off this isn't at all described in appearance or whatever in the description. I mean yeah sure you're not supposed to know until later ra ra spookiness but it's an important damn thing you can at least recognize its significance in the description!

why do they keep peeling my skin it hurts make it stop it hasn't stopped for hours HELP ISN'T COMING no matter what he says help isn't coming.

Okay, this is South Korea right? I assume they're writing in Korean? If Korean is anything like Chinese or Japanese, this is a horrible and shitty translation because of the capitalizations there. I'm pretty sure there isn't a different symbol for capitalizations of a word, just words put together. The only reason that you would capitalize 'help isn't coming' is that they physically write it huge compared to everything else in the sentence. Also, the beginning isn't capitalized. Again, since Korean as far as I assume doesn't work like that, they're either speaking is some pretty great english for a crazy Korean or this is very much bullshit.

good ideas are bad ideas i have a very good idea they can't collect on what isn't here. empty threats when nothing can be worse than this i dare them.

DITTO. Especially with the 'i' and the lack of capitalizations here. It pisses me off, because that's not how the language works, so you can't use a lack of capitalization to portray anything. That's like me trying to portray English in a manner the language just plain doesn't work as, such as writing it backwards because some other language reads right-to-left and assumes English does too.

so nice of you to come i can sing they like to sing we'll all never stop singing not even when we can't blink anymore and the lights flash and flash and flash. i know a song and i like parties all kinds all places. i know two songs the ones they write for me and the ones THEY write for me. i don't like songs.

I'm so angry and pissed off at this. Just because it's easier to portray a crazy man by fucking his grammar, doesn't mean you get to just ignore how grammar works in other languages. Is this still translated from Korean? Am I getting Korean wrong? Are they writing in English? They write in pretty good fucking English if they are.

"lava tube" structure

Either state where this is quoted from, or drop the quotes altogether. Otherwise it eeks out tone for me.

D-88127, carrying the video camera, crawled first into the passage, followed by D-49213.

Four commas in one sentence. I don't like it. Surely it can be split with a semicolon or a sentence or something? Even an 'and' would help.

what turned out to be a shaft with an estimated depth of 25 m

'turned out to be' is awkward tone for me.

subjects documented their surroundings, revealing that the walls

'revealing' is also very awkward tone. Maybe better as '…subjects documented their surroundings. Most notably, the walls…'

In the center of the chamber, subjects discovered

Tone tone 'discovered' annoys me. Maybe better as 'subjects found'.

a twin-screw extruder machine with an integrated computer terminal, such as that seemingly described in the SCP-1538 narrative (described in later reports as SCP-1538-1). SCP-1538-1 was wired to a series of automotive batteries, which served as an apparent improvised power source. No other artificial structures appeared to be present in the chamber.

So why isn't this in the description? It's hinted at, but it's important enough to be given its own designation. It should be mentioned sooner, or at least the fact its significant mentioned in the description.

Upon instructions from researchers, D-49213, having been briefed on basic functions of extruder machinery prior to the mission, approached the computer terminal and activated SCP-1538-1

This sentence is a hot mess. Maybe it would be better as 'D-XXXX approached the computer terminal and activated SCP-XXXXX-1 upon instruction from researchers'. Because I see no reason why 'having been briefed on the functions of extruder machinery prior to the mission' needs to be in that sentence or in the SCP at all. It's info we don't need. Cut the fat.

Other than that, sounds really cool and weird, but I can't get over the insane guy's shitty not-making-any-korean-to-english-sense and the shitty structure of the article itself, because you would think that something THAT significant would get fucking front and center in the article or at least a mention that 'HEY THIS IS IMPORTANT'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still have my face :<…

But seriously, well done, author. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*claps*
One of the first articles that has creeped me out in a while. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it.

Though, I will say that the human-slurry pseudopod with a face on the end felt a bit… blatant, compared to the preceding material. Not enough to make me consider downvoting, but it was a bit jarring.

If that's what you were going for, then good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pretty damn creepy, though I won't lie: The ending reminded me VERY much of the "Enigma of Amigara Fault."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the amount of detail you put into it. Very well thought out little touches that make for good reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very worth the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very unique, indeed. A machine referenced in suicide notes that creates face monsters of the suicide victims? I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty long, but it was a good read. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a nice and creepy, semi-surreal SCP. I feel that sometimes the suicide notes use a bit too much technical jargon in relation to that twin-screw extruder machine thing, but the ominous juxtaposition of the jargon with the expressions of personal anguish by the people more than makes up for any confusion I might have felt otherwise.

And that Expedition summary was amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
guuh

That was fucking creepy. The tension was palpable as I went through the Summary of Expedition 1538-1.

Upvoted, this is now one of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of the suicide notes is very strong, and they're delightfully creepy to read, since it feels so wrong to have people's personal emotional messages hijacked by that thing. The cave system exploration was also well-handled; I was legitimately wondering what creepy thing awaited at the end, and your detailed description made it feel like we were actually inching through, building up for the revelation. Also, I like that this is set in South Korea; the SCP foundation is a global one, after all.

The human face though and ensuing chase though… Eh. I think just pumping the slurry with human parts out is frightening enough. It also seems unlikely that the D-Class would not be forthcoming with details; I imagine he would be yelling something along the lines of "Oh my God, Vinnie just got his face eaten off by a giant purple people eater!"

Still, this is way more entertaining than anything I can write. Upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it up until halfway through the addenda. Making it an 'thing what kills you by spraying dead peoples faces' kind of ruins the fun for me. It would be much better if it was just a piece of industrial equipment that for some reason is communicating its manual by manipulating suicides.

Hesitant upvote, but I think you can make it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hmmm….interesting
